sourceMovie.title: Green Street Hooligans
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Green Street Hooligans (2005) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Lexi Alexander's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Green Street Hooligans clicked for you, 21 (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Street Kings
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Green Street Hooligans clicked for you, Street Kings (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Green Street Hooligans?
faq.answer: Try 21, Street Kings, God Bless America—each shares DNA with Green Street Hooligans while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Green Street Hooligans?
faq.answer: Green Street Hooligans (2005) is best described as Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Green Street Hooligans part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Green Street Hooligans Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
